name: Hazard Pointers

x.coordination: A lock-free reclamation scheme: a thread publishes pointers it is about to dereference as "hazardous"; memory is freed only once no thread lists it as hazardous.
hazards: Bounded number of hazard pointers per thread
Reclamation latency vs memory usage trade-off

def: A safe-memory-reclamation technique for lock-free structures: threads announce the nodes they are accessing so those nodes are not freed until no one references them.
problem: In lock-free structures, a thread may hold a pointer to a node another thread wants to free — freeing it causes use-after-free (the core SMR problem).
mechanism: Each thread has a small set of hazard-pointer slots. Before dereferencing a shared node it stores the pointer in a slot and re-validates. A reclaimer scans all threads’ hazard slots and only frees nodes that appear in none, deferring the rest.
limits: Fixed number of protectable pointers per thread
failures: Publishing the hazard after the dereference (UAF window)
Missing a memory fence between publish and recheck
whenNot: Read-mostly data where RCU/epoch is cheaper
Code that can use GC or reference counting acceptably
myths: "Lock-free means no memory management problem" — reclamation is the hard part, which hazard pointers solve
